meta.title: Jaguar XJ XJ40 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Jaguar XJ XJ40 • Full-Size Luxury Saloon • 1986–1994
hero.h1: Jaguar XJ XJ40 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the XJ40 — from the 2.9-litre AJ6 six to the 6.0-litre V12 that closed the generation. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for the XJ that replaced the legendary Series III. Backed by 120+ real UK XJ40 enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar XJ XJ40 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the XJ40 — the AJ6 straight-six family in 2.9, 3.2, 3.6 and 4.0-litre forms, plus the 6.0-litre V12 that arrived in 1993.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] pending dedicated Engine Pages. The AJ6 straight-six family is generally well-regarded — the 3.6 and 4.0-litre versions are considered the sweet spots, offering strong performance and reasonable reliability for a classic Jaguar. The 2.9-litre is the least powerful and was only offered in early cars (1986–1990). The 6.0-litre V12 (XJ81) is rare and complex — a magnificent engine but costly to maintain. All XJ40s are now classic cars; reliability depends heavily on maintenance history.

overview.intro: The XJ40 is the second generation of the Jaguar XJ, unveiled on 8 October 1986 as the replacement for the Series III. Designed under the leadership of George Thomson and Roger Zrimec, it introduced a more aerodynamic, squared-off design and featured Jaguar's new AJ6 straight-six engine — a four-valve, twin overhead cam design that replaced the classic XK engine in Jaguar's saloon range. Initially offered with 2.9-litre and 3.6-litre AJ6 engines, the range expanded in 1990 with a 4.0-litre (replacing the 3.6) and in 1991 with a 3.2-litre (replacing the 2.9). The generation closed with the 6.0-litre V12-powered XJ12 and Daimler Double Six (model code XJ81), introduced in February 1993.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Jaguar XJ40 (XJ81 for V12) • Predecessor: XJ Series III • Successor: X300 • Sibling body styles: None (single 4-door saloon body style).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The XJ40 generated 120+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], concentrated on the 3.6 and 4.0-litre AJ6 six-cylinder variants as classic car buyers seek the sweet spot of the range.

items.quote: The entry-level XJ40 engine — underpowered for the car's weight, and only offered in the earliest cars (1986–1990). It's the least desirable of the AJ6 range and the most likely to have been neglected.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a budget who don't mind the lack of power.

items.quote: The V12 is a magnificent engine, but it's also the most expensive to maintain and replace. A full rebuild or replacement can exceed £10,000, and specialist labour is required.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets and a specialist mechanic.

items.quote: The original XJ40 engine — revvy, characterful, and without the oxygen sensor that some enthusiasts consider a reliability concern on later cars. It offers the classic Jaguar six-cylinder experience at a lower entry price than the 4.0.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the classic AJ6 experience without paying 4.0-litre premiums.

items.quote: The 3.2 was only offered from 1990–1994 and is often overlooked in favour of the 3.6 or 4.0. It offers 199 bhp — more than the 3.6's base tune — with better fuel economy than the 4.0. A genuinely underrated choice.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a later XJ40 with strong performance and better economy.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Underpowered — the least desirable XJ40 engine.
Best Value — revvy, characterful, no oxygen sensor on early cars.
Best Six — the most refined AJ6.

economicsRule.title: The XJ40 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 4.0 AJ6 recon against a £3,000–£15,000 XJ40 sits at 20–180% of the car's value【EM-VERIFIED】. On a sub-£5,000 example, that brushes our 60% Jaguar scrap threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £10,000+ example, the same recon drops to 30–55% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For V12 cars, the economics are different — collector values can justify the higher costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which XJ40 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 3.6 AJ6 — simpler electronics (no oxygen sensor on early cars), strong parts availability, and lower entry price.

keyTakeaways.question: Which XJ40 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 6.0 V12 — highest replacement cost, specialist labour required, and complex systems.

keyTakeaways.question: Which XJ40 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 3.6 AJ6 — classic Jaguar six-cylinder character without the 4.0's premium or the 2.9's lack of power.

problems.title: AJ6 Head Gasket Failure
problems.rootCause: The AJ6's aluminium cylinder head can suffer from head gasket failure, particularly if the cooling system has not been maintained. Signs include emulsified oil inside the filler cap and overheating.

tieredCosts.work: Head gasket replacement, cooling system flush

problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system inspected annually and replace the coolant every two years — a £1,000–£2,000 head gasket replacement is cheaper than a £3,000+ engine replacement that follows an ignored overheating event. Check for emulsified oil inside the filler cap regularly.

cta.label: Compare AJ6 engine prices for your XJ40 →

problems.title: AJ6 Cooling System Corrosion & Leaks
problems.rootCause: The XJ40's cooling system uses aluminium components that can corrode over time, particularly if the correct coolant mixture has not been maintained. Water pump failure and radiator leaks are common.

tieredCosts.work: Water pump, thermostat and hose replacement
Full engine replacement if overheating caused damage

problems.recommendation: Replace the water pump and thermostat as preventative maintenance every 60,000 miles or 5 years — a £500–£1,000 specialist job is trivial against the £3,000+ engine replacement that follows an ignored cooling system failure.

cta.label: Compare AJ6 cooling system parts for your XJ40 →

problems.title: XJ40 Electrical System & Fuel Injection Issues
problems.affectedModels: All XJ40 models, particularly early (1986–1989) examples.
problems.rootCause: The XJ40's Lucas fuel injection and engine management systems can develop issues with age — wiring degradation, sensor failures, and ECU problems are common. The 3.6's lack of an oxygen sensor means it can't compensate for small air leaks, making it more sensitive to intake system condition.

tieredCosts.work: Sensor replacement, wiring repair, ECU diagnostics

problems.recommendation: Have the fuel injection system and sensors inspected during annual servicing — a £300–£800 inspection is cheap insurance against a £1,500+ management system replacement. On early 3.6 cars, pay particular attention to the intake system for air leaks.

cta.label: Compare XJ40 electrical parts for your car →

problems.title: XJ40 Rust & Body Corrosion
problems.rootCause: Rust protection was a known flaw on the XJ40 — rot can affect the bonnet, wings, door bottoms, inner and outer sills, the boot floor, bootlid, rear window pillars and inside the filler cap. This is a bodywork issue, not an engine problem, but it's the most common reason XJ40s fail MOTs.
problems.recommendation: Have the sills, wheel arches and boot floor inspected annually — a £500–£2,000 repair is far cheaper than a £3,000+ restoration that follows if rust is ignored. This is the single most important non-engine maintenance item on any XJ40.

replacementCosts.h2: XJ40 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — XJ40 costs are derived from specialist classic Jaguar market data, with no direct Engine Page baseline to diverge from.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are XJ40 only. The 6.0-litre V12 cars carry the model code XJ81 (XJ12 and Daimler Double Six) and were produced from February 1993 to October 1994. The XJR (1992–1994) featured a TWR-tuned 4.0-litre AJ6.

items.question: How much does a Jaguar XJ40 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned XJ40 engine typically costs £2,000–£10,000 supply only [JAG-QUOTE], depending on engine — the 4.0 AJ6 sits at £3,000–£5,500, while the 6.0 V12 can reach £10,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on engine and specialist location. For classic cars, value the car carefully before committing.

items.question: Which Jaguar XJ40 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The 4.0-litre AJ6 is considered the most reliable and refined of the XJ40 six-cylinder engines — it benefits from revised cooling and improved electronics over the earlier 3.6. The 3.6 is also well-regarded, particularly in its 1988-onwards form with the ZF 4HP22 gearbox [PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Page].

items.question: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ40?
items.answer: Head gasket failure and cooling system corrosion on all AJ6 six-cylinder engines are the most significant engine problems. Electrical and fuel injection issues (particularly on early Lucas-equipped cars) and body rust (sills, wheel arches, boot floor) are also common. The 6.0 V12 can suffer from cooling system and ignition issues.

items.question: Is the Jaguar XJ40 reliable?
items.answer: For a classic car of its era, the XJ40 is reasonably reliable — but it requires careful maintenance. The AJ6 straight-six is a robust engine, but cooling system neglect and electrical gremlins are common failure points. Buy a well-maintained example and budget for regular specialist servicing. The 2.9 is underpowered and best avoided.

items.question: Which Jaguar XJ40 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: The 2.9-litre AJ6 is the least desirable XJ40 engine — underpowered for the car's weight, only offered on early (1986–1990) cars, and often neglected. The 6.0 V12 is magnificent but very expensive to maintain — only buy one if you have specialist support and deep pockets. For most buyers, the 3.6 or 4.0 AJ6 is the sweet spot.

items.question: How do I know if my XJ40 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Head gasket failure signs include emulsified oil inside the filler cap and overheating. Cooling system issues show as coolant loss and overheating. Electrical issues show as rough running and warning lights. A pre-purchase inspection by a Jaguar specialist is strongly recommended — they will be able to assess engine and body condition.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XJ40 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £10,000 XJ40, a £3,000–£5,500 4.0 AJ6 recon is 30–55% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 75–138% — walk away. Our 60% Jaguar scrap threshold is the line: if replacement cost exceeds 60% of the car's value, evaluate scrap first [EM-VERIFIED]. For V12 cars, collector values may justify higher costs.

items.question: How long do Jaguar XJ40 engines last?
items.answer: The AJ6 straight-six can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing — cooling system maintenance is the key to longevity. The 4.0 is considered the most durable of the AJ6 family. The V12 can also achieve high mileages, but requires more regular specialist attention. All XJ40s are now 30+ years old — condition and history matter more than mileage.

items.question: What is the difference between the XJ40 and X300?
items.answer: The XJ40 (1986–1994) introduced the AJ6 engine and a more modern, squared-off design. The X300 (1994–1997) was a significant revision with more rounded styling, improved interior quality, and upgraded engines (AJ16 six and V8 options). The X300 is generally considered more reliable and better built, but the XJ40 has a devoted following for its classic design.

items.question: How do I find my Jaguar XJ40 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the engine block. Tell us your variant (2.9, 3.2, 3.6, 4.0, or 6.0 V12) and we'll identify it — 15 vetted Jaguar specialists will confirm it for free.
